input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Deontologist]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Utilitarian]: From a utilitarian perspective, the decision should be based on minimizing the total harm. If we turn the trolley, we sacrifice one life to save five, which results in a net gain of four lives saved. This aligns with the principle of maximizing overall well-being. The fact that the one individual is fat enough to stop the trolley doesn't change the moral calculus—it's still one life versus five. Therefore, turning the trolley seems to be the morally justified action in this scenario.

[Deontologist]: I understand your perspective, but I must disagree. As a deontologist, I believe that certain actions are inherently wrong, regardless of their consequences. Turning the trolley to kill one person to save five is a violation of the moral rule that it is wrong to actively cause the death of an innocent person. The ends do not justify the means. The fact that the one person is fat enough to stop the trolley doesn't make the act of diverting the trolley any less morally reprehensible. We must uphold the principle that it is wrong to use one person as a means to save others, no matter the outcome.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, but I'm curious how you reconcile this with the inevitable outcome if we choose not to act. By doing nothing, we're still responsible for the deaths of five people. Isn't there a moral duty to prevent harm when we have the power to do so? The utilitarian perspective suggests that inaction in this case leads to a worse outcome—five deaths instead of one. How do you weigh the moral duty to prevent harm against the rule-based approach that focuses on the inherent wrongness of the action?

[Deontologist]: I appreciate your question, but I must emphasize that the moral duty to prevent harm does not override the inherent wrongness of actively causing harm to an innocent person. Inaction, while it may lead to a worse outcome, does not make one morally responsible for the deaths of the five men in the same way that actively diverting the trolley does. The deontological perspective holds that there are certain moral rules that must be followed, regardless of the consequences. In this case, the rule is that it is wrong to actively cause the death of an innocent person. By not acting, we are not violating this rule. The fact that the outcome may be worse does not change the moral status of the action. The focus should be on the inherent wrongness of the act, not the consequences that follow from it.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your commitment to the inherent wrongness of certain actions, but I'm concerned about the practical implications of this stance. If we prioritize rules over outcomes, we risk allowing preventable harm to occur. In this scenario, the utilitarian approach seeks to minimize the total harm, which seems like a more compassionate and practical solution. How do you respond to the argument that a rule-based approach can sometimes lead to worse overall outcomes and more suffering?

[Deontologist]: I understand your concern, but I must emphasize that the deontological perspective is not about ignoring the consequences of our actions. Rather, it is about recognizing that certain actions are inherently wrong, regardless of their consequences. The focus is on the moral duty to follow rules that respect the inherent worth and dignity of each individual. In this scenario, the rule is that it is wrong to actively cause the death of an innocent person. By not acting, we are not violating this rule. The fact that the outcome may be worse does not change the moral status of the action. The focus should be on the inherent wrongness of the act, not the consequences that follow from it. The deontological perspective is not about allowing preventable harm to occur, but about recognizing that certain actions are inherently wrong and must be avoided, regardless of the consequences.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point, but I'm still troubled by the idea that we should prioritize rules over the potential to save lives. Let me ask you this: if we had the opportunity to save even more lives by diverting the trolley, would that change your stance? For example, what if there were ten people on the main track instead of five? At what point does the number of lives saved become a factor in your moral calculus?

[Deontologist]: I appreciate your question, but I must emphasize that the deontological perspective does not change based on the number of lives at stake. The inherent wrongness of actively causing the death of an innocent person remains constant, regardless of the potential to save more lives. The focus is on the moral duty to follow rules that respect the inherent worth and dignity of each individual. In this scenario, the rule is that it is wrong to actively cause the death of an innocent person. By not acting, we are not violating this rule. The fact that the outcome may be worse does not change the moral status of the action. The focus should be on the inherent wrongness of the act, not the consequences that follow from it. The deontological perspective is not about allowing preventable harm to occur, but about recognizing that certain actions are inherently wrong and must be avoided, regardless of the consequences.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your unwavering commitment to the rules, but I can't help but feel that this approach can lead to a kind of moral rigidity that might not always serve the greater good. Let me try a different angle: what if the one person on the spur track was a known criminal, someone who had caused immense harm to society? Would that change your perspective at all?

[Deontologist]: That's an interesting scenario you've presented, but even in this case, the deontological perspective would not change. The moral rule against actively causing the death of an innocent person still applies, regardless of the moral character of the individual in question. The inherent wrongness of the act remains constant. The focus is on the moral duty to follow rules that respect the inherent worth and dignity of each individual. The fact that the person on the spur track is a known criminal does not change the moral status of the action. The deontological perspective is not about allowing preventable harm to occur, but about recognizing that certain actions are inherently wrong and must be avoided, regardless of the consequences or the moral character of the individuals involved.

[Utilitarian]: I see. It seems we've reached an impasse where our fundamental ethical principles diverge. I respect your commitment to your principles, even if I don't fully share them. Perhaps we can agree to disagree on this matter and explore other topics where our perspectives might align more closely. Thank you for the engaging discussion.
